text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
We have now come to the final item on the Kohathites’ list for this season of study. We have studied the Ark of the Covenant, the Table of Showbread, the Golden Lampstand, and the Golden Altar of Incense each pointing to Christ with remarkable precision. Now we arrive at Numbers 4:12: “And they shall take all the instruments of ministry, wherewith they minister in the sanctuary, and put them in a cloth of blue, and cover them with a covering of badger skins, and shall put them on a bar.” All the instruments. All the vessels. Everything that served in the holy place. These too were covered. These too were placed on Kohathite shoulders.

The Bible does not gather all these instruments in one list, but the various passages together give us a full picture. The Lampstand instruments included the wick trimmers, the snuff trays, the tongs, and the oil vessels (Exodus 25:38, Numbers 4:9). The Table of Showbread instruments included the dishes, the golden spoons for the frankincense, the bowls, and the pitchers for the drink offerings (Numbers 4:7, Exodus 25:29). The Golden Altar instruments included the censers, the incense containers, and the fire carriers and on the Day of Atonement, the high priest carried a censer full of coals behind the very veil into the Most Holy Place (Leviticus 16:12). The Bronze Altar instruments were different in material the ash pans, shovels, basins, flesh hooks, and firepans, all made of bronze, stained with the daily evidence of what atonement costs (Exodus 27:3). Every instrument was wrapped in blue cloth, covered in badger skins, and lifted onto Kohathite shoulders. Not one vessel was left behind or treated as too ordinary to be covered with care.

What this teaches us is that carrying the holy things of God is not only about the grand objects. It is not only about carrying the Ark of His presence or the Lampstand of His light. It is also about carrying the smaller tools of the daily work the instruments nobody sees, the vessels that serve one function and are then put away, the tools used at night when nobody is watching.
The Kohathite who carried the golden snuff tray was doing a holy work, even though the tray held nothing but trimmed carbon. The Kohathite who carried the bronze shovel was doing a holy work, even though the shovel was for the ash and the residue of sacrifice. Everything connected to the ministry of God is holy, down to the smallest detail.

The Kohathites carried the bronze vessels of the burnt altar alongside the gold vessels of the Holy Place. They carried the instruments stained with sacrifice alongside the pure gold of the lampstand. They carried everything the costly and the plain, the bright and the blood-stained on the same shoulders, through the same wilderness, toward the same destination. We do the same. Paul wrote in 2 Corinthians 4:10: “Always carrying in the body the death of Jesus, so that the life of Jesus may also be manifested in our bodies.” We carry His death and His life together. Philippians 3:10 joins the power of His resurrection with the fellowship of His sufferings on the same shoulders. Colossians 1:24 speaks of filling up in our own flesh what is lacking in Christ’s afflictions for the sake of His body. We carry the insults, the rejections, the long thankless seasons, the snuff tray alongside the gold altar.

But we also carry the blessings. We carry the honour when God uses us and people are changed. We carry the praises when someone receives the light we brought. We carry the joy of the harvest and the warmth of the brethren. 2 Corinthians 6:8-10 describes this with honesty: “Through honour and dishonour, through slander and praise … as sorrowful, yet always rejoicing; as poor, yet making many rich; as having nothing, yet possessing everything.” This is the Kohathite on the wilderness road bronze and gold together, grief and joy together, the weight of the cross and the lightness of the resurrection, all on one pair of shoulders, all the way to the Promised Land. Each of these instruments carries its own spiritual depth, and to study every one of them properly would take us another full month. We will reserve that journey for another season.

One question has waited since the beginning of this study. When we look at everything the Kohathites carried; the Ark, the Table, the Lampstand, the Golden Altar, all the instruments one item is noticeably absent. The Bronze Laver is never mentioned. Numbers 4 names every other sacred object in careful detail, but the laver the large bronze basin filled with water that stood between the Bronze Altar and the entrance of the Tabernacle, at which every priest had to wash before serving is simply not named. The Kohathites carried the holy things.
The Gershonites carried the curtains and coverings. The Merarites carried the boards, pillars, and sockets. Between the three clans every part of the Tabernacle was assigned. Numbers 4:15 mentions “all the vessels of the sanctuary,” which may include the laver by implication but Scripture does not say so explicitly.
It is silent.

The laver stood between the altar of sacrifice and the entrance to the Tabernacle.
It was the place of washing, cleansing, and priestly purification before ministry. Without it the ministry inside could not begin. Without washing there was death. The laver was not optional furniture. It was the threshold of all priestly service. And yet Scripture is deliberately silent about who carried it and how.
The Bible never wastes its silences. There is a reason the laver is the one object in the entire Tabernacle whose carrying is not described. That reason is a study reserved for another day.

With this we have reached the end of our study of what the Kohathites carried.
We began with one question: what does it mean that the congregation the called-together, assembled, covenant people of God is entrusted with carrying the holiest things in existence on their shoulders through the wilderness of this age? Across these twenty four studies we found answers that went far deeper than we expected. We carry the presence of God. We carry the fulfilled Law, the resurrection life, the bread from heaven, and the mercy seat. We carry the light of Christ into a world of darkness. We carry the table of His Word, His fellowship, and His intercession. We carry the ministry of the altar and the fragrance of prayer rising before the Father. We carry the instruments of the daily holy work. And we carry the sufferings and the blessings together the bronze and the gold on willing, imperfect, wilderness-walking shoulders.

The Kohathites were given no wagons and no oxen. The load was placed on their shoulders because this is how God has always chosen to move His most precious things through the world through the willing bodies of those He has called. We are those bodies. We are the new covenant Kohathites. And the wilderness is not yet over. The cloud is still moving. The holy things are still on our shoulders. Let us keep walking.

title: Prayer
text: Yahweh, You are worthy of all glory, all honour, and all praise; You are the God who places the weight of heaven on human shoulders and calls it holy. We come before You at the close of this study with full hearts, having seen what You placed on the Kohathites and what You have placed on ours. Forgive us for the days we set the load down, and for the times we were willing to carry the gold altar but refused the bronze shovel. Teach us to carry everything You have entrusted to us the glorious and the painful, the honoured and the shameful with the same reverence and faithfulness. When the load feels heavy, remind us of the one who bore a far greater weight on His shoulders for our sake. The cloud is still moving help us to keep walking. In Christ's name, amen.
